findings.title: Read-range planning now leaks a positional tuple contract into every readAllData caller
findings.why: The refactor moves prop scanning out of `readAllData`, but the replacement API is a `[][2][]uint64` tuple plus two extra slice parameters. That makes each caller decode magic indexes and keep four correlated slices aligned by convention instead of by type.
findings.risk_if_unchanged: Future callers can easily swap the tuple indexes, mix offsets from different key boundaries, or pass misaligned slices into `readAllData`, which makes this hot path harder to review and easier to regress with subtle partial-range reads.
findings.evidence: `getReadRangeFromProps` now returns `[][2][]uint64`, and callers unpack it with positional indexing such as `readRanges[0][0]` and `readRanges[1][1]` before passing `startOffsets` and `endOffsets` into `readAllData`. The same unpacking pattern now appears in `merge_v2.go`, `testutil.go`, `reader_test.go`, and `bench_test.go`.
findings.change_request: Why not use a small domain type or focused helper here? Keep the prop-derived offsets encapsulated as a named read-range object, or provide a single-range helper so callers do not need to understand `[start/end][per-file]` indexing.
